title: The case for keeping minor museums small
summary: Argue whether small, single-subject museums should expand to attract larger audiences or deliberately remain modest in scale.
topic: Whether small museums should resist the pressure to grow
prompt: Write a 350–500-word argumentative essay about whether modest, specialised museums — a one-room museum of typewriters, a two-floor museum of municipal water supply — should expand their collections, build new wings, and chase visitor numbers, or whether they should deliberately stay small. Use a cleft sentence, a concession ('Granted, …' or 'Admittedly, …'), at least one passive construction, and one participle clause. Reference at least one named city, museum, or programme. Take a position.
hintStarters: What a small museum does well is something that no expansion programme has ever managed to…
Granted, the funding model for cultural institutions in the post-pandemic period has placed enormous pressure on…
Tucked into a side street in Krakow, a single-room museum of mechanical clocks demonstrates how a deliberately limited collection can…

hintVocab.meaningVi: bộ sưu tập chuyên đề
lượng khách ghé / số lượt người đến
chạy theo số lượt khách

trọng tâm trưng bày / sưu tập
làm loãng một bộ sưu tập
trưng bày thân mật / gần gũi
bảo tàng chuyên đề đơn nhất
sự bảo trợ của cộng đồng / chính quyền địa phương
trợ cấp hoạt động
doanh thu từ cửa hàng lưu niệm
chiều sâu hơn là bề rộng
mở rộng quy mô chương trình
sự nhất quán theo chủ đề
bị trung gian hóa quá mức (qua màn hình, hướng dẫn)
thiết bị hướng dẫn âm thanh
văn bản giới thiệu treo tường (trong bảo tàng)
nhà tài trợ từ thiện
vẫn duy trì khả năng tài chính
khoản tài trợ để mở rộng
khiêm tốn về quy mô

sampleText:
The instinct of almost every institution is to grow. A successful restaurant adds tables; a thriving school adds students; a museum that begins to attract attention is told, soon enough, that it ought to expand. Larger collections, longer queues, a second floor, a café with table service, an audio guide in twelve languages. Growth, in the cultural sector as elsewhere, is taken to be self-evidently a good thing.

It is worth asking what is lost when a small museum decides to become a large one. What a single-subject museum does well is hold an entire subject in the visitor's attention for an hour or two — a manageable, complete encounter. Tucked into a side street in Krakow, the small museum of mechanical clocks demonstrates this principle clearly. Three rooms, perhaps two hundred objects, and a curator who is often the same person who built half the display cases. By the end of a visit, you understand how a clock works. You will not understand this after three hours in a national gallery of horology, however well funded.

Granted, the funding model for cultural institutions in the post-pandemic period has placed enormous pressure on small museums, and many have closed. Operating subsidies have been cut, gift shop revenue cannot bridge the gap, and the philanthropic donors who underwrite serious expansion are interested in serious expansion, not in maintenance. The economic argument for growth is, in many cases, an economic argument for survival.

Even so, the survival framing tends to flatten the choice. Many of the small museums that have closed in the last decade did so not because their original model failed but because they were persuaded, often by well-meaning consultants, that the model was no longer enough. New wings were built that were never filled. Audio guides were commissioned that no visitor wanted. The collection, having been diluted to fill the expanded space, lost the thing that had made the original museum worth visiting.

What needs defending here is not nostalgia but a particular form of cultural work — the kind that depends on intimacy, on a single voice in the wall text, on the visitor encountering an object closely rather than processing it from across a room. That work is genuinely threatened by the institutional growth imperative.

A small museum that stays small is not failing to fulfil its potential. It is, more often, fulfilling exactly the potential it was built for. The question worth asking before any expansion grant is accepted is whether the museum will still be itself on the other side of the renovation.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: It is worth asking what is lost when a small museum decides to become a large one
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Đáng để hỏi điều gì bị mất đi khi một bảo tàng nhỏ quyết định trở thành một bảo tàng lớn

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to isolate the small museum's particular value
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What a single-subject museum does well is hold an entire subject in the visitor's attention for an hour or two' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' để định nghĩa chính xác giá trị đặc biệt mà mô hình nhỏ mang lại. Cấu trúc này thay vì viết 'A single-subject museum holds an entire subject…' giúp tách bạch hành động độc đáo của bảo tàng nhỏ ra như một mệnh đề riêng được nhấn mạnh.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What a single-subject museum does well is hold an entire subject in the visitor's attention for an hour or two' uses a what-cleft to pinpoint the small museum's distinctive value. Rather than 'A single-subject museum holds an entire subject…', the cleft isolates the unique action as its own emphasised clause.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Perfect participle clause to express prior cause
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'The collection, having been diluted to fill the expanded space, lost the thing that had made the original museum worth visiting' dùng mệnh đề phân từ hoàn thành bị động ('having been diluted') để diễn tả một nguyên nhân xảy ra trước hành động chính. Cấu trúc này nén toàn bộ chuỗi nhân quả vào một câu mà không cần đến hai mệnh đề riêng biệt nối bằng 'because'.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'The collection, having been diluted to fill the expanded space, lost the thing that had made the original museum worth visiting' uses a perfect passive participle clause ('having been diluted') to mark a cause that preceded the main action. The structure compresses a full causal sequence into one sentence without needing two separate clauses joined by 'because'.

sampleTranslationVi:
Bản năng của hầu hết các tổ chức là tăng trưởng. Một nhà hàng thành công thêm bàn; một trường học phát đạt nhận thêm học sinh; một bảo tàng bắt đầu thu hút sự chú ý sẽ sớm được khuyên rằng nó nên mở rộng. Bộ sưu tập lớn hơn, hàng dài hơn, tầng thứ hai, một quán cà phê có phục vụ bàn, một hướng dẫn âm thanh bằng mười hai ngôn ngữ. Sự tăng trưởng, trong lĩnh vực văn hóa cũng như những nơi khác, được coi là điều hiển nhiên tốt.

Đáng để hỏi điều gì bị mất đi khi một bảo tàng nhỏ quyết định trở thành một bảo tàng lớn. Điều mà một bảo tàng chuyên đề đơn nhất làm tốt là giữ trọn một chủ đề trong sự chú ý của khách tham quan trong một hoặc hai giờ — một cuộc gặp gỡ có thể quản lý được, trọn vẹn. Nằm khép mình trong một con phố phụ ở Krakow, bảo tàng nhỏ về đồng hồ cơ học minh họa nguyên tắc này một cách rõ ràng. Ba căn phòng, có lẽ hai trăm hiện vật, và một người quản lý trưng bày thường là chính người đã làm nửa số tủ trưng bày. Đến cuối chuyến tham quan, bạn hiểu cách một chiếc đồng hồ hoạt động. Bạn sẽ không hiểu được điều này sau ba giờ trong một viện bảo tàng quốc gia về đồng hồ học, dù được tài trợ tốt đến đâu.

Thừa nhận rằng, mô hình tài trợ cho các tổ chức văn hóa trong giai đoạn hậu đại dịch đã đặt áp lực to lớn lên các bảo tàng nhỏ, và nhiều bảo tàng đã đóng cửa. Trợ cấp hoạt động đã bị cắt, doanh thu cửa hàng lưu niệm không thể lấp đầy khoảng trống, và các nhà tài trợ từ thiện tài trợ cho việc mở rộng nghiêm túc chỉ quan tâm đến việc mở rộng nghiêm túc, không phải bảo trì. Lập luận kinh tế cho việc tăng trưởng, trong nhiều trường hợp, là một lập luận kinh tế cho sự sống còn.

Dẫu vậy, khung diễn giải sống còn có xu hướng làm phẳng sự lựa chọn. Nhiều bảo tàng nhỏ đã đóng cửa trong thập kỷ qua không phải vì mô hình ban đầu của họ thất bại mà vì họ đã bị thuyết phục, thường bởi những cố vấn có thiện chí, rằng mô hình ban đầu không còn đủ nữa. Các cánh phòng mới được xây nhưng không bao giờ được lấp đầy. Hướng dẫn âm thanh được đặt làm nhưng không khách tham quan nào muốn. Bộ sưu tập, sau khi bị làm loãng để lấp đầy không gian mở rộng, đã đánh mất điều đã làm cho bảo tàng ban đầu đáng để ghé thăm.

Điều cần được bảo vệ ở đây không phải là sự hoài niệm mà là một hình thức công việc văn hóa đặc biệt — loại phụ thuộc vào sự gần gũi, vào một giọng nói duy nhất trên văn bản treo tường, vào việc khách tham quan tiếp xúc với hiện vật ở khoảng cách gần thay vì xử lý nó từ bên kia căn phòng. Công việc đó thực sự bị đe dọa bởi mệnh lệnh tăng trưởng của tổ chức.

Một bảo tàng nhỏ giữ mình nhỏ không phải là thất bại trong việc phát huy tiềm năng. Thường thì, nó đang phát huy chính xác tiềm năng mà nó được xây dựng cho. Câu hỏi đáng đặt ra trước khi chấp nhận bất kỳ khoản tài trợ mở rộng nào là liệu bảo tàng có còn là chính nó ở phía bên kia của việc cải tạo hay không.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to isolate the small museum's particular strength?
options: Holds well a single-subject museum what does for the visitor an hour or two attention.
What a single-subject museum does well is hold an entire subject in the visitor's attention for an hour or two.
A single-subject museum what well holds is an entire subject in the visitor's attention.
An entire subject what holds in the visitor's attention a single-subject museum does.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What [subject + verb phrase] is [verb phrase]' is the cleft pattern; the most important content sits after 'is'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is …' with the new information delayed to the end.

options: A loud sound from inside a museum gallery
The quality of being unified around a single subject or theme
The bright lighting used to display valuable objects
The use of multiple languages on display labels
mcQuestions.explanation: Thematic coherence means that everything in a museum (or any work) holds together around a clear, single theme; it is the opposite of being scattered.
mcQuestions.hint: Break the phrase apart: 'thematic' (about themes) + 'coherence' (holding together).

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'granted' correctly as a concession opener?
options: Granted, the funding model for cultural institutions in the post-pandemic period has placed enormous pressure on small museums.
The funding model granted has placed enormous pressure small museums in the post-pandemic period.
Granted in the post-pandemic period the funding model small museums on enormous pressure has placed.
Enormous pressure small museums on granted has placed the funding model in the post-pandemic period.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted,' followed by a complete clause cleanly opens a concession to the opposing argument before the main case is reasserted.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places 'Granted,' before a full, well-formed sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a perfect participle clause to express a prior cause?
options: The collection, having been diluted to fill the expanded space, lost the thing that had made the original museum worth visiting.
Having lost the collection diluted to fill the expanded space had the thing made worth visiting.
Worth visiting the collection lost the thing made having been diluted to fill expanded space.
Diluted having been the collection to fill expanded space lost the thing worth visiting made.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'having been diluted to fill the expanded space' is a perfect passive participle clause expressing a cause that came before the main action ('lost the thing…').
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'having been + past participle' as a clause that explains a cause preceding the main verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to dilute a collection' mean?
options: To add water to artworks for cleaning purposes
To weaken the focus or quality of a collection by adding less important items to fill space
To translate a collection's catalogue into a simpler language
To distribute the collection across several smaller venues
mcQuestions.explanation: Dilute in this context means weaken by adding extraneous material — the original focus is lost when too many lesser items are added.
mcQuestions.hint: What happens to strong coffee when you add lots of water? Apply that to a museum's contents.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the passive to foreground the building work rather than its agent?
options: Consultants built new wings that were never filled.
New wings were built that were never filled.
The new wings were what builders did that no one filled.
Builders new wings constructed which they never filled.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'New wings were built that were never filled' uses the passive twice, foregrounding the buildings (what was done) rather than the agents (who did it). It also delivers the essay's irony more efficiently.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option makes 'new wings' the subject of the sentence?

options: A legal duty to expand whenever profits allow
An unspoken assumption that institutions must grow to be considered successful
A government policy requiring all museums to expand
A grant that pays specifically for growth
mcQuestions.explanation: An imperative is a strong, often unstated demand; the 'institutional growth imperative' is the assumption that organisations must continually grow to count as healthy.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'imperative' as 'something you feel you must do' — what does an institution often feel it must do?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best paraphrases 'the survival framing tends to flatten the choice'?
options: When the question is framed as survival, the real range of options gets squeezed out of view.
Cultural institutions usually survive only when their choices are flat.
Survival is the most important consideration for any small museum.
Flattened museums are easier to survive in than tall ones.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Flatten the choice' means reduce it to a single dimension; if the only question is 'grow or die', other options (staying the same, downsizing intentionally, restructuring quietly) become invisible.
mcQuestions.hint: What does it mean to 'flatten' a decision so that all the options disappear except one?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a return to the main argument after a concession?
options: For example,
Even so, the survival framing tends to flatten the choice.
On the contrary, no museum has ever closed.
Similarly, growth is always best.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' grants the concession just made (funding pressures are real) and then pivots back to the main argument that the survival framing is itself problematic.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both accepts the previous concession and reasserts the writer's position?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay suggest is the strongest argument for staying small?
options: Small museums are cheaper to insure than large ones.
Small museums let visitors encounter an entire subject closely in a single, manageable visit.
Small museums attract more international tourists per square metre.
Small museums are unlikely to be targeted by thieves.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's central claim is that small museums offer a 'manageable, complete encounter' with a subject — something a large national gallery cannot replicate.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the second paragraph, which describes what the small Krakow museum achieves.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: Tiny museums are basically way cooler than big ones because you actually learn stuff.
Honestly big museums are kind of overwhelming so small ones win.
The intimacy of a small specialised museum supports a kind of attentive encounter with material that is difficult to replicate at scale.
Small museums are great and people should support them more, full stop.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses precise vocabulary ('intimacy', 'attentive encounter', 'difficult to replicate at scale') and avoids contractions, slang, and informal hedges.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'basically', 'kind of', 'full stop', and other colloquial markers.

options: A one-time payment to start a new museum
Recurring public or institutional funding that covers the routine costs of running an organisation
An entrance fee paid by foreign visitors
A subsidy paid to surgeons working in museum hospitals
mcQuestions.explanation: An operating subsidy is regular funding for ongoing operations — salaries, utilities, maintenance — not for capital projects or expansions.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what 'operating' costs are, as opposed to 'capital' costs.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a defining relative clause?
options: A curator who is often the same person who built half the display cases.
Often the same person a curator who built half the display cases who is.
Half the display cases who built often the same person a curator is who.
Built the display cases half who often the same person is a curator who.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'who is often the same person who built half the display cases' is a defining relative clause specifying which kind of curator the essay means.
mcQuestions.hint: Defining relative clauses follow the noun immediately and start with 'who', 'that', or 'which'.

options: A person who donates to political campaigns
A wealthy individual or foundation that gives money for the public good
A guide who works in a museum without pay
A donor of medical equipment to hospitals
mcQuestions.explanation: Philanthropy is the giving of money or resources for public benefit; a philanthropic donor is someone who funds public-good causes such as museums.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Philanthropy' comes from Greek roots meaning 'love of humanity' — what kind of donor would that suggest?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best closes the essay by reframing the choice an institution faces?
options: In conclusion, all museums are good in their own way.
Therefore, no museum should ever expand under any circumstances.
The question worth asking before any expansion grant is accepted is whether the museum will still be itself on the other side of the renovation.
To sum up, small museums and large museums each have things to offer visitors.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C reframes the choice in a single, memorable question that captures the essay's central concern — identity through change.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option ends with a specific, pointed question rather than a vague summary?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay argue is genuinely at risk when small museums expand?
options: The financial solvency of the city's tourism sector
The kind of close, intimate cultural encounter that small museums uniquely offer
The careers of consultants who advise on expansion
The market for audio guides in multiple languages
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's central concern is the specific cultural value — close, attentive, single-voiced — that gets lost when scale grows.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the fifth paragraph, which describes 'what needs defending here'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a small museum, gallery, or cultural space you have visited that you remember well. What made the visit memorable — and would the experience have survived if the venue had doubled in size? What specifically would have been lost?
